title: Vitamin C and iron absorption
source: Proceedings of the Nutrition Society (systematic review and meta-analysis)
text: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) helps the body take up non-heme iron, the form found in plant foods, by converting it into a form that is easier to absorb. Pooled studies show that adding vitamin C to a meal significantly raises the share of iron absorbed from that meal, which matters most for people eating mainly plant-based or with higher iron needs. The benefit is on absorption within a meal: taking vitamin C alongside oral iron does not clearly improve long-term haemoglobin beyond iron on its own. So vitamin C may support how well iron is absorbed, rather than being a guaranteed fix for low iron levels.
